topic: EXP-256 falsifiability-module integrated on Overview tab — adversarial review of the integrated section
change_summary: EXP-256 integrated as a new <section class="falsifiability-module"> on Overview, with two open follow-ups (ISS-1580 WCAG, ISS-1581 asymmetric framing). This review checks the rendered, integrated content rather than the proposal artifact (which was reviewed in EXP-256-proposal.json under queue_id 267).
our_argument_summary: The Overview tab now exposes a 4-row "What would change our verdict" module immediately after the verdict bar chart and before the EXP-261 thesis-hero. It surfaces the four conditions from the eg-falsifiability section (discriminating prediction; genuine prospective prediction; derive 7.83 Hz from dome geometry; sub-5% coordinates without globe inputs) with statuses 0/69, 1 partial (eclipse Aug 12, 2026), 0/69, pending (OPEN-001). The fm-jump anchor links readers to the full eg-falsifiability prose. The integration is faithful to the proposal patches: 1× <section class="falsifiability-module">, 4× <li class="fm-row">, all four condition titles present verbatim, fm-jump href="#eg-falsifiability" present, all colors via CSS tokens (--refuted-solid, --accent, --ink-3) — no hardcoded hex.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: counts.total = baseWins.length where baseWins = wins.filter(w => /^\d{3}$/.test(w.id)). Currently resolves to 69 (3-digit IDs only — excludes sub-claim collisions). Status numerators (0, 1, 0, pending) are static literals in the template; only the denominator 69 is computed. The verdict bar chart uses wins.length = 71 as denominator. Two different denominators on the same Overview screen.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Numerators are hardcoded; denominator (69) is computed from baseWins.length. The "1 partial" eclipse status is hardcoded — there is no live check against any prediction file.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None directly in the module. The fm-jump link delegates context to eg-falsifiability prose, which contains the existing eclipse pre-commitment language and the WIN-044/WIN-057 attempt count.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None for the module itself. However, the linked eg-falsifiability prose (line 663 of docs/index.html) describes the August 12, 2026 eclipse as "annular" — Section 2.7 elsewhere on the same page (line 2935) correctly describes it as "total, not annular" with NASA Besselian magnitude 1.0386. This is a factual contradiction inside the surface the module routes readers to.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: This is a section, not a WIN. monitor.py does not contain logic for the falsifiability-module. The status values are hardcoded in the build template.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Not applicable — this is review-side prose, not a dome claim.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Not applicable — the module is review content, not a prediction.

holes_found.description: Denominator mismatch on the Overview tab is now visually adjacent. The verdict bar chart aria-labels read "Misleading: 22 of 71 claims (31.0%)" etc. (N=71, includes sub-claim collisions). The falsifiability module immediately below reads "0 / 69" twice (N=69, base 3-digit WINs only). A casual reader and especially a screen-reader user will hear two different denominators back-to-back with no inline explanation. The proposal flagged this distinction as intentional and consistent with §6.5, but on the rendered page the two denominators sit ~10 lines apart with nothing bridging them. This was raised as a minor in the c1 proposal review but the side-by-side rendering elevates it to moderate.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a single-line caption note under the module clarifying the 69-vs-71 distinction, e.g., "Across the 69 distinct dome claims (sub-claims aggregated)." Alternatively, normalize both to the same denominator (either 71 with footnote or 69 with bar chart adjustment). Lowest-cost fix: small parenthetical on the fm-caption.

holes_found.description: Missing test coverage. The proposal explicitly listed five test_additions (assert class="falsifiability-module" exactly once; exactly 4 fm-row elements; all four condition titles present; fm-jump href="#eg-falsifiability"; positional check that the module appears AFTER verdict-bars). None of these were added to test.js — grep for "falsifiability-module|fm-row|fm-jump|fm-condition" in test.js returns zero matches. Per CLAUDE.md "Expanding a page with a new numeric claim → add a test for it," the four condition titles and the 0/69 / 1 partial / pending statuses are exactly the kind of claims that should be testable. A future build pipeline change or template restructure could silently drop the module and CI would not catch it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the five proposal-spec test assertions to test.js. Estimated work: ~15 lines of grep-style assertions following the existing test patterns. File a follow-up issue if it is not done in the next decider self-apply pass.

holes_found.description: Linked content in eg-falsifiability has a factual error that the module now drives more traffic to. Line 663 of docs/index.html describes the August 12, 2026 eclipse as "annular solar eclipse" — but Section 2.7 (line 2935 same file) correctly describes it as "total, not annular" using NASA Besselian magnitude 1.0386. The actual August 12, 2026 eclipse is total (Iceland, Greenland, NW Spain). The annular eclipse of 2026 was February 17 (Antarctic). The module itself does not assert annular vs total — it just says "eclipse Aug 12, 2026" — but its fm-jump anchor lands readers directly on the paragraph containing the error. EXP-256 did not introduce the error, but it is now structurally a feeder to it. A defender could use "you do not even know what kind of eclipse it is" as a credibility attack on the broader review.
holes_found.recommendation: File a follow-up issue (or fold into a content patch) to change "annular solar eclipse" → "total solar eclipse" at docs/index.html line 663 (i.e., in build-scripts/generate-html.js source for the eg-falsifiability eclipse pre-commitment paragraph). Cross-check: Section 2.7 on the same page already states the correct value, so this is a localized typo fix, not a substantive analytical change.

holes_found.description: "0 / 69" framing for condition 1 conflates "did not attempt" with "attempted and failed." The eg-falsifiability prose explicitly says "only 2 of 69 claims produce a quantitative dome-specific derivation (WIN-044, WIN-057), and both fail." So 67 of the 69 did not even meet the precondition for condition 1 (a quantitative dome-specific derivation), and 2 of 69 met the precondition and produced wrong values. The display "0 / 69" reads as if all 69 had a fair shot and all 69 failed — which is rhetorically vulnerable. A dome defender can argue: "You are penalizing 67 claims for not attempting condition 1 even though condition 1 was an opt-in standard, not a requirement." This is the same critique flagged as minor in the c1 proposal review and as the underlying issue in ISS-1581 (asymmetric framing). The integrated module did not adopt either Option A (footer explanation) or Option B (consistent framing) — it just shipped with the issue tracked.
holes_found.recommendation: Resolve ISS-1581 by adding a brief inline note ("of the 69 claims, 2 attempted condition 1; 0 met the bar") or by reframing as "0 confirmed (2 attempted)" / "0 / 2 attempted". The current form is defensible as visual consistency but vulnerable on the merits.

holes_found.description: Integrated visual order has thesis-hero AFTER falsifiability module rather than before. The proposal's hero_composition_check assumed a sequence of [thesis (4rem) + bar chart (16rem) + module (16rem) ≈ 36rem] with the thesis on top. After EXP-261 v2 was integrated (queue_id 273), the actual order is bar chart → falsifiability module → thesis-hero → 5-question preface. Readers see "What would change our verdict" with concrete conditions BEFORE they see the explicit thesis sentence "None of the 69 predictions produces a dome-specific result that standard physics cannot already explain." This is unusual composition — most pages state the conclusion first and then the falsification standard. It is defensible (distribution → standard → thesis as resolution) but the proposal's above-the-fold calculation no longer matches reality. EXP-256 itself is fine; the layered EXP-255/EXP-261 hero rework changed the surrounding context.
holes_found.recommendation: Treat as informational. If anyone reopens hero composition: consider whether the thesis-hero should move above the bar chart (canonical claim/result/standard sequence) or whether the current order (distribution → criteria → thesis) is the intended pedagogical rhythm. No action required for EXP-256 itself.

holes_found.description: Open follow-up ISS-1580 (dark-mode WCAG contrast) is still open at moderate severity. The fm-status-num red on --card-bg dark mode computes to ~4.43:1, just below WCAG AA 4.5:1 for normal text at 0.95rem bold. The decider chose to land EXP-256 with the issue tracked rather than block on it. This is a defensible product choice but is now visible on the live site for any user on dark mode.
holes_found.recommendation: Track ISS-1580 to closure. Either bump fm-status-num to a size that crosses the large-text threshold (1.05rem+ bold, 14pt) or use a more saturated --refuted-solid token in dark mode. The existing token review proposal is correct; this is a normal queued accessibility cleanup.

holes_found.description: "1 partial — eclipse Aug 12, 2026" awards partial credit to a prediction whose data has not yet arrived (eclipse is in ~3.5 months from review date). The framing is grounded in the "approach this standard but have methodological issues" wording in the eg-falsifiability prose, but a strict reading would be "1 pending" until the eclipse occurs and the magnetometer data is in. Crediting partial NOW commits us to a position before the data lands. Defensible (acknowledges the dome's real if methodologically flawed effort) but a defender could argue we have already locked in our verdict cosmetically.
holes_found.recommendation: Acceptable as-is. After the August 12, 2026 eclipse data is available, update the status atomically: "1 confirmed" if the test passes, "0 / 69" if it fails, with the timestamp-error caveat preserved on either path. The current "partial" label should not survive past the eclipse date in either direction.

holes_found.description: Class names do not follow the codebase's ds- prefix convention (other Overview elements: ds-thesis-hero, ds-tf-item, ds-ep-row, ds-vb-bar). The module uses falsifiability-module, fm-row, fm-status-zero, etc. — a bare-prefix style that breaks naming consistency. Already flagged in the proposal review as moderate; the integrated build kept the bare names. This is cosmetic but has token/grep ergonomics cost.
holes_found.recommendation: Optional: queue a low-priority sed pass to rename to ds-falsifiability-module / ds-fm-row / ds-fm-status-* — matches existing token discipline. Not blocking.

stronger_arguments.description: When/if the asymmetric framing fix lands (ISS-1581), consider explicit per-condition denominators rather than a uniform 69. Condition 1: "0 confirmed / 2 attempted". Condition 3: "0 attempted" (since no dome derivation of 7.83 Hz exists at all). Condition 4: "pending — V12/V13 in progress (OPEN-001)". This both preempts the "unfair denominator" defender attack and communicates the actual structure of attempts vs failures. The module would still read at-a-glance: anything other than 0 or pending is a direction-of-travel signal.

The dome could legitimately respond "register a discriminating prediction tomorrow and you will upgrade us next week." To make that path concrete, consider a brief note in the fm-caption such as "Register a prediction at any time; we will re-grade within 7 days of public commitment." This signals cooperative grading rather than ex post facto judgment.

kernel_of_truth.description: The module materially closes a real gap in the prior Overview: until EXP-256 landed, a casual reader could click through the entire Overview without ever seeing what would change our verdict. The four conditions are real falsification criteria, not gerrymandered ones — derive from dome geometry, register prospective predictions, derive 7.83 Hz, build coordinates without globe inputs are all things a dome model legitimately should be able to do if it is right. The fact that the module reports 0/69, 1 partial, 0/69, pending is structurally honest because it states the criteria publicly and lets the dome target them.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The presence of clear falsification criteria does not by itself prove the verdicts are correct — those still rest on the per-WIN evidence in Part 3 and the kill-shot tests in Part 5. The module is a discoverability win, not a probative one. It strengthens the review's claim to falsifiability without changing any underlying evidence.

deeper_analysis: EXP-256 is a discoverability fix, not a substance fix. The four conditions already lived on a separate Evaluate Guide tab and the previous Overview did not surface them. After integration, every Overview read makes the falsification standard public above-the-fold-adjacent, which preempts the strongest single defender attack ("this review is unfalsifiable"). The module does this without inventing new conditions or rephrasing the source prose in a load-bearing way. Three concerns survive integration: (1) the side-by-side denominator mismatch (71 vs 69) on the same Overview — already flagged but worth a single-line caption fix; (2) the asymmetric framing of "0/69" vs "pending" without inline disambiguation, tracked in ISS-1581; (3) test coverage gap — the proposal listed five test_additions and none were added to test.js, leaving the integration unprotected against template regression. The eclipse "annular" factual error in the linked eg-falsifiability prose is a separate finding the module exposes by routing traffic to that paragraph. Visual hierarchy with thesis-hero appearing after the module is a layered consequence of EXP-261 being integrated after EXP-256, not a defect of EXP-256 itself. Advocate-mode rebuttal: a dome defender's strongest line is the denominator-conflation argument ("you are scoring 67 claims that did not attempt condition 1 as failures"). Ranking 3/5 — the module text is technically correct but rhetorically vulnerable; a single inline footnote forecloses the defense. Quantitative verification: counts.total resolves to 69 (baseWins.filter for 3-digit IDs in current data/wins.json), 0 of those 69 produce a confirmed condition-1 demonstration (per Part 3 verdicts), and 2 of those 69 (WIN-044 Self-Contradicted, WIN-057 Misleading at 6.2% RMSE) attempted. Cross-WIN consistency: the eg-falsifiability prose, Section 1.3 (V13 coordinates), §6.5 (denominator distinction), and Part 5 kill-shots all use the 69-claim base consistently. The bar chart at 71 is the outlier (includes sub-claim collisions) and that distinction is documented elsewhere on the page but not adjacent to the module.

citations_verified: fm-jump href="#eg-falsifiability" — id present at docs/index.html line 649
baseWins.length = 69 — verified from data/wins.json filter logic
Section 2.7 confirms August 12, 2026 eclipse is total (NASA Besselian magnitude 1.0386)
citations_failed: eg-falsifiability eclipse pre-commitment paragraph (line 663) describes the August 12, 2026 eclipse as "annular" — actual eclipse is total. Existing site error, not introduced by EXP-256, but module fm-jump now feeds traffic to it.

advocate_mode.defense_voice: A reasonable dome defender would say: (a) Your falsifiability module credits us with a partial on the eclipse before you have eclipse data — thank you, but please commit to upgrading to confirmed if the August 12 measurements fall in our predicted range, not retreating to a methodological-issues caveat. (b) You score "0 / 69" on conditions 1 and 3, but only a small number of claims even attempted those conditions; 67 claims were never about deriving novel discriminating quantities — they were about explaining existing observations. Penalizing them for not meeting condition 1 is moving the goalposts. (c) You credit us "pending" on condition 4 only because we publicly flagged OPEN-001 ourselves. A model that did not honestly publish open problems would not get this label, so transparency is being used against us. (d) The module sits between your bar chart and your thesis statement — a visual sequence that primes readers toward your conclusion before they see your reasoning.
advocate_mode.defense_assessment: Defense (b) is the strongest — the denominator-conflation reading of "0 / 69" is real and we should preempt it. Defense (a) is foreclosed by the eg-falsifiability eclipse pre-commitment paragraph (which already commits us to upgrade if the dome's prediction beats Chapman). Defense (c) cuts both ways — public OPEN-001 acknowledgment is good practice but does not by itself constitute progress; tracking is preserved by the sub-status. Defense (d) is layered consequence of EXP-261 ordering, not a defect of EXP-256.

summary_for_decider: Integration is faithful to the proposal patches. Three moderate findings worth chasing: (1) denominator mismatch 71/69 visible side-by-side on Overview, fix with one-line fm-caption note; (2) missing test coverage — five test_additions from proposal were never added to test.js, file a follow-up; (3) eclipse "annular" factual error in linked eg-falsifiability prose at line 663, change to "total" — module did not introduce it but routes more traffic to it. Two open issues (ISS-1580 dark-mode WCAG, ISS-1581 asymmetric framing) remain valid and tracked correctly. Layered visual hierarchy with thesis-hero now AFTER the module is a layered consequence of EXP-261 v2 ordering, not an EXP-256 defect — flag for operator attention but no action required on this queue item. Recommended action: minor_edit (denominator caption + tests), with ISS-1580/ISS-1581 closure on normal cadence and a separate one-word typo fix for the annular/total error.
